facts.text: The United States declared independence from Great Britain in 1776.
The Declaration of Independence was adopted on July 4, 1776.
The U.S. Constitution was written in 1787 and remains the oldest written national constitution still in use.
George Washington became the first president of the United States in 1789.
The Louisiana Purchase of 1803 nearly doubled the size of the young nation.
The Lewis and Clark Expedition explored the newly acquired western territory from 1804 to 1806.
The American Civil War lasted from 1861 to 1865 and centered primarily on slavery and the preservation of the Union.
Abraham Lincoln issued the Emancipation Proclamation on January 1, 1863.
Reconstruction followed the Civil War and attempted to rebuild the South and define freedom and citizenship after slavery.
The transcontinental railroad was completed in 1869, linking the country by rail from coast to coast.
The Industrial Revolution transformed the United States in the late 19th and early 20th centuries through factories, railroads, and mass production.
The Great Depression began after the stock market crash of 1929 and reshaped American politics and economic policy.
Franklin D. Roosevelt's New Deal created sweeping federal programs in response to the Great Depression.
The U.S. entered World War II after the attack on Pearl Harbor on December 7, 1941.
The Civil Rights Movement of the 1950s and 1960s challenged segregation and expanded legal protections for Black Americans.
The Voting Rights Act of 1965 became one of the most important civil-rights laws in U.S. history.
Apollo 11 landed on the Moon in 1969, marking a major Cold War and scientific milestone for the United States.
The Watergate scandal led to President Richard Nixon's resignation in 1974, the only presidential resignation in U.S. history.
The United States has repeatedly expanded through migration, innovation, conflict, reform movements, and constitutional debate.
U.S. history is often studied through the tension between national ideals and the struggle to make those ideals real for everyone.
The Declaration of Independence announced the American colonies' break from British rule in 1776.
It remains one of the most important documents in U.S. political history.
Declaration of Independence is one of the most important topics associated with U.S. History.
Books, documentaries, and classrooms that cover U.S. History frequently highlight Declaration of Independence.
Learning about Declaration of Independence helps explain a major part of U.S. History.
For many readers, Declaration of Independence stands out as one of the defining subjects in U.S. History.
The story of Declaration of Independence is closely tied to the broader themes of U.S. History.
Declaration of Independence is often used to introduce students to the history and significance of U.S. History.
Many surveys of U.S. History return again and again to the importance of Declaration of Independence.
From public memory to scholarship, Declaration of Independence plays a notable role in how people understand U.S. History.
One reason U.S. History remains so compelling is the lasting significance of Declaration of Independence.
The legacy of Declaration of Independence continues to shape how U.S. History is discussed today.

The Declaration of Independence announced the colonies’ break from British rule in 1776.

Declaration of Independence is one of the first topics that comes up when people explore U.S. History.
Learning about Declaration of Independence gives a stronger feel for the depth and variety inside U.S. History.
Books, documentaries, and reference guides on U.S. History often highlight Declaration of Independence.
Declaration of Independence remains one of the most widely recognized examples connected with U.S. History.
For many readers, Declaration of Independence is a memorable entry point into U.S. History.
The story of Declaration of Independence helps explain why U.S. History stays so interesting to learn about.
Declaration of Independence is frequently mentioned in discussions, lessons, and articles about U.S. History.
One reason U.S. History feels so rich is that it includes major touchpoints like Declaration of Independence.
Readers interested in U.S. History often come across Declaration of Independence very quickly.
Declaration of Independence still stands out as a classic reference point within U.S. History.
